California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 26, 1986


Walter J. Egan
505 East Lomita Boulevard, #1
Carson, CA 90745

Re: A-86-058

Dear Mr. Egan:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act has been received by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice
request, you may contact the Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division at (916) 322-5662.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or unless more information is needed to answer your request,
you should expect a response within 21 working days.

Very truly yours,

Jeanne Pritchard
Chief
Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division

JP:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


March 27, 1986

Walter J. Egan
505 East Lomita Blvd., #1
Carson, CA 90745

Re: FPPC No. A-86-058

Dear Mr. Egan:

This is in reply to your letter dated February 11, 1986,
addressed to Robert Leidigh, concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

You enclosed with your letter a copy of a letter and a
survey which were sent by you to the citizens of Carson. You
indicated that the costs of the mailing were paid for by a
foundation established by your mother, and asked us to determine
whether or not the survey is a "non-political activity."

The Political Reform Act of 1974 provides that all
"contributions" received by, and all "expenditures" made by a
candidate or an officeholder must be disclosed on the candidate's
or officeholder's campaign disclosure statements.

The Fair Political Practices Commission's regulation 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18215 provides that a "contribution" is a
payment which is:

...received by or made at the behest of:

a candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding
circumstances that the payment was received or made at
his or her behest for personal purposes unrelated to
his or her candidacy or status as an officeholder.
(Emphasis added)

FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225 provides that
an "expenditure" is a payment which is:

...made by a candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was made
for personal purposes unrelated to his or her
candidacy or status as an officeholder. (Emphasis
added)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Walter Egan
March 27, 1986
Page 2


     Based on these definitions, it is clear that the costs of the
letter and survey you sent to the citizens of Carson are
reportable.

     In addition, it appears from the information provided on the
"Exemption Application" for the Walter J. Egan Education
Foundation which you enclosed, that the foundation may meet the
definition of a "committee" under Gov. Code Section 82013(a). We
believe the foundation meets the definition of a committee because
some of the moneys received by the foundation appear to meet the
definition of "contributions." In addition, at least some of the
payments made by the foundation are "expenditures" (the payments
for the costs of the letter and survey are "expenditure"). If
the foundation meets the definition of a committee under Gov. Code
Section 82013(a), it must file a Statement of Organization (Form
410) and campaign disclosure statements.

     Please call me at (916) 322-5662, and we can discuss the
obligation of the foundation to register and file reports as a
committee.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Chief, Technical Assistance
                                        and Analysis

JP:kt

FEB 14 9 19 AM '86

505 East Lomita Boulevard #1
Carson, California 90745
February 11, 1986


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California    95804

Attention of Mr. Robert Leidigh:

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

I am writing you in response to a telephone conversation that I had with Helen Arriola, Deputy Field Officer for the Los Angeles F.P.P.C. office on Monday, February 10, 1986.

During our conversation we discussed a survey that was recently sent to the community. I am enclosing within this letter a copy of that survey as well as a copy of the answer that was sent back to members of the community who so requested a response.

You will also find a copy of a newspaper article that gives the survey responses from a former survey in the form of a recap on its front page.

Some time ago my mother, Rosie T. Egan, wished to establish a foundation that would endeavor to cover and report the costs of non-political activities. Upon seeking the advice of an attorney, a foundation was formed.

I am sending you a copy of the first project that the foundation has been involved with. In discussing the merits of this program, it has been suggested to me that I seek the advice of the Fair Political Practices Commission on whether or not this would be considered non-political activity.

I, therefore, have called Helen Arriola and she has indicated to me that surveys are normally evaluated on a program by program basis.

Since I have just finished sending in my annual F.P.P.C. report to the City Clerk (July 1, 1985 to December 31, 1985), I want to make sure that I have done everything to comply with the law. Therefore, I am respectfully requesting your opinion on whether or not the subject survey is a non-political activity.

Most sincerely,

Walter J. Egan

The Foundation personally contacts individuals and businesses in Carson. A campaign
by mail or newspaper is not anticipated. Fund raising committees most likely will
not be used. The Foundation does not intend to use professional fund raisers.
The Foundation does not have any written solicitations for financial support.

March 16, 1985

(Signature)                                                            (Title or authority of signer)                                (Date)

Rosie T. Egan                                                         President

Dear Citizen of Carson:

As your City Councilman I have solicited your thoughts and opinions on civic projects. This method of communication has brought an outstanding number of replies which has given me the opportunity to hear your concerns. It has also been very helpful to me in the development of City policies which are of benefit to our community.

The implementation of a sign program for citywide street sweeping is just one example. Another community suggestion was the removal of old, broken, unsightly bus benches with the replacement of the blue benches that are now in use.

Many of the questions that you will find on the reverse side have been suggested by citizens who have expressed their views at meetings and civic events. I meet many of you in the community and I am always pleased to hear your opinions and ideas. I find your input very valuable when I am called upon to make decisions that affect the City of Carson. With this idea in mind, I am sending this survey to expand my contact with those of you who wish to share your ideas and concerns. I am asking for your opinion and I would appreciate a few minutes of your time to let me know what you feel about your city and the direction you would like it to take.

I am looking forward to hearing from each and everyone of you. If I can be of service to you in any way, please feel free to give me a call.

Most sincerely,

[illegible signature]

WALTER J. "JAKE" EGAN
City Councilman

Survey questions on reverse side.........

Printed by Jacobsen Printers, Carson, California, and is not paid for at taxpayers' expense.

1985 SURVEY QUESTION RETURN

1) The City of Carson recently cosponsored a FOURTH OF JULY CELEBRATION
utilizing the velodrome at Cal State Dominguez Hills. Former survey responses
indicate support for such programs. Would you be in favor of my continuing to
notify survey responders of future community events so that you many have the
opportunity to participate?

YES    93%         NO    7%

2) There are many methods the City uses to disperse citizen information. What
source do you or members of your family use to learn about City activities?

a) City Newsletter                                           67%
b) Local newspapers                                          17%
c) Direct communication from an elected official              11%
d) Community Building Signboard                              2%
e) Other                                                     3%

3) Are you aware that the City of Carson annually sponsors a float in the Pasadena
Tournament of Roses Parade?

YES  92%              NO    8%

4) Would you, or a member of your family, enjoy participating with other Carson
citizens in decorating our float for this years parade?

YES  31%              NO    69%

5) Past survey respondents have indicated support for the recently implemented
sign program for citywide street sweeping. Now that the program has been
implemented, are you finding the benefits of this new program to be an asset
in your neighborhood?

YES  80%              NO    20%

6) Are there any suggestions that you feel would make an improvement to the new
citywide street sweeping program in your area?

a) Enforcement of ticketing for street sweeping program.              63%
b) Coordination of street sweeping and trash pickup on same day.      24%
c) Would like to see the street sweeper slow down.                   9%
d) Favored both sides of street to be swept on same day.             8%

7) One of the most important concerns expressed by citizens in our community is
aesthetics. What specific suggestion would you have to improve the aesthetic
quality of our City?

a) Favored cleanup of commercial/business areas.                     43%
b) Wanted the removal of abandoned vehicles from lawns and
   driveways. [illegible]                                            39%
c) Suggested street sweeping of business areas.                      18%

8) Recent State legislation (SB-1091) is being proposed that would return to non-
property tax cities, such as Carson, a portion of property taxes collected by
the County of Los Angeles. Would you be in favor of returning this rebate
directly to Carson property owners?

YES  87%              NO    13%

9) The City of Carson has three (3) Sister Cities. They are Robertsport, Liberia;
Sinait, Philippines; and Soka, Japan. Would you be interested in participating
in a Pen Pal program in any one of these cities?

YES    29%            NO    71%

10) Would you like to have the tabulated results of this survey returned to you? If
so, please indicate below.

YES  87%              NO    13%

Citizens Express Views
Survey of 10,600 Voters Unveils Feelings

Carson Bulletin 10¢
And Wilmington Beacon

Your Community Newspapers
Equal Opportunity Employers

VOL. 10 NO. 5    642 E. CARSON ST., CARSON, CA. 90745    WEDNESDAY, OCTOBER 13, 1982    774-0018 or 549-6351

Councilman Walter J. "Jake" Egan, conducted his own survey of a substantial cross section of Carson residents, on areas citizens concerns, and came up with some startling answers.

As an example, the questionnaire results showed that only 25 percent of the residents rated the city's progress as "excellent."

The majority, 53 percent, replied that the profile was "fair," and a surprising 22 percent registered "poor."

The survey pointed out that the city council has appropriated a substantial amount of money for construction of the new Community Building, approximately $12,900,000.

The question: "Do you think the Community Building should be used to house social service programs?"

Two-thirds of the residents surveyed answered yes, 68 percent in favor and 32 against.

Another question on the community building pointed out that it is estimated that $300,000 a year will be needed to maintain the building. Egan questioned: "Do you think we should close parts of the building when they are not in use, in order to bring the buildings maintenance costs down?"

An overwhelming number of cost-conscious residents, 79 percent were in favor. According to nine percent, no. Of those polled, the remainder did not know the answer, nine percent no-answered that the cost of the survey was borne by friends of Egan, and one cost taxpayers expense.

In his preface, Egan stressed that "I have never believed that an elected official can sit in an office and come up with the answers to increasingly difficult problems. For this reason I have sought the personal contact that many of you through the personal contact that you have. I find that I do not know your views on all of the subjects included in this survey."

Egan explained that "for subjects that do not lend themselves to a simple answer, I have included an 'Open Question.'"

Included in the Carson resident questionnaire were the following:

"In your opinion, what area of city service needs the most improvement?"

Carson citizens replied to nine questions in this poll. Three percent were paid to nine percent, no. Of those polled, the remainder did not know the answer.

Only one percent of the citizens answered that city buildings were inadequately maintained. Many point out that the city does not own buildings but rents from individuals. The questionnaire pointed that previously the city had seen the practice in the past.

Four opinions were included in a survey question that asked "In your opinion, what should the city of Carson be faced with major cutbacks due to the loss of revenue sharing, county, and federal funds, what areas of service would you recommend we cut?"

The survey pointed out that 76 percent of satisfied customers, however, only 10 percent were in favor of a once a week trash pickup.

The survey pointed out that presently 20 percent of the trash pickup service users do not pay their trash bills. According to the survey, "consolidated billing" would reduce this factor and provide "savings for the city." Sixty-two percent of the residents polled prefer the proposed billing system. The city's service billing with the county billing system, if this would reduce costs that would be passed on to the customer, one hundred percent of the cross-section of residents were in favor of buying memberships of the city council set specific hours for hearing citizen concerns. The questionnaire pointed out the former meetings of the city council had the policy of opening their council office to the public.

The great majority, 79 percent were in favor of putting the axe to city council and department expenses.

Of those queried, 76 percent of the residents said they would prefer the present trash service in Carson. One-fourth said no. Of the 76 percent of satisfied customers, however, only 10 percent were in favor of a once a week trash pickup.

The survey pointed out that presently 20 percent of the trash pickup service users do not pay their trash bills. According to the survey, "consolidated billing" would reduce this factor and provide "savings for the city." Sixty-two percent of the residents polled prefer the proposed billing system. The city's service billing with the county billing system, if this would reduce costs that would be passed on to the customer.

The survey pointed out that the corner of 223rd and Moneta is a proposed city recreation parksite. Sixty-four percent of those queried were against cutting the site in half to provide low income housing or a relocation mobile home park.

Fifty-four percent of the citizens surveyed also turned thumbs-down on the proposal of promoting a relocation mobile home park or low-income housing in the Avalon Greens area.

However, by a slight margin, 52 to 48 percent did not mind if low-income housing or mobile home relocation were sited on the site at Central and 190th Street.

The survey also unveiled the ideas, 56 to 44 percent, of promoting a mobile home park or low-income housing at 285th and Main.

Egan's survey revealed a messier pie in the sky project.

His survey questionnaire went out to 10,600 of the Carson residents who voted in the 1980 city council election.

The number of "returns" were as startling as some of the answers.

Normally, he said, a survey is answered only by 10 to 20 percent of the persons polled.

"The Egan survey netted a 17 percent reply."

Egan 505 East Lomita Boulevard, #1 Carson, CA 90745 Re: A-86-058 Dear Mr. Egan: Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act has been received by the Fair Political Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request, you may contact the Technical Assistance and Analysis Division at (916) 322-5662. We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or unless more information is needed to answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 working days. Very truly yours, Jeanne Pritchard Chief Technical Assistance and Analysis Division JP:plh 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 California Fair Political Practices Commission March 27, 1986 Walter J. Egan 505 East Lomita Blvd., #1 Carson, CA 90745 Re: FPPC No. A-86-058 Dear Mr. Egan: This is in reply to your letter dated February 11, 1986, addressed to Robert Leidigh, concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. You enclosed with your letter a copy of a letter and a survey which were sent by you to the citizens of Carson. You indicated that the costs of the mailing were paid for by a foundation established by your mother, and asked us to determine whether or not the survey is a "non-political activity." The Political Reform Act of 1974 provides that all "contributions" received by, and all "expenditures" made by a candidate or an officeholder must be disclosed on the candidate's or officeholder's campaign disclosure statements. The Fair Political Practices Commission's regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 provides that a "contribution" is a payment which is: ...received by or made at the behest of: a candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was received or made at his or her behest for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an officeholder. (Emphasis added) FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225 provides that an "expenditure" is a payment which is: ...made by a candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was made for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an officeholder. (Emphasis added) 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660 Walter Egan March 27, 1986 Page 2 Based on these definitions, it is clear that the costs of the letter and survey you sent to the citizens of Carson are reportable. In addition, it appears from the information provided on the "Exemption Application" for the Walter J. Egan Education Foundation which you enclosed, that the foundation may meet the definition of a "committee" under Gov. Code Section 82013(a). We believe the foundation meets the definition of a committee because some of the moneys received by the foundation appear to meet the definition of "contributions."
